steps.title: Read the configuration from the figure
steps.content: From the figure, points $R$, $V$, $U$ lie on one straight line with $V$ between $R$ and $U$. Point $T$ lies above that line, and $S$ lies on segment $RT$ (between $R$ and $T$). The angle $\angle VST = 23^\circ$ is the angle at $S$ inside triangle $SVT$ (between ray $SV$ and ray $ST$, where ray $ST$ continues up segment $RT$ toward $T$). The angle $\angle RVS = 34^\circ$ is at $V$ between ray $VR$ and ray $VS$. The asked value is $x = \angle UVT$, the angle at $V$ between ray $VU$ and ray $VT$.

steps.title: Apply the exterior angle theorem to triangle SVT at vertex V
steps.content:
Because $R$, $V$, $U$ are collinear with $V$ between $R$ and $U$, rays $VR$ and $VU$ are opposite rays. The angle $\angle RVS = 34^\circ$ is the exterior angle of triangle $SVT$ at vertex $V$ (it is formed by extending side $TV$ through $V$ to $R$ and meeting side $SV$). The exterior angle theorem states that this exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles:
$$\angle RVS = \angle VST + \angle VTS$$
$$34^\circ = 23^\circ + \angle VTS$$
$$\angle VTS = 11^\circ$$
Since $S$ is on segment $RT$, ray $TS$ is the same as ray $TR$, so $\angle VTS = \angle VTR = 11^\circ$.

steps.title: Apply the exterior angle theorem to triangle RVT at vertex V
steps.content:
Now consider triangle $RVT$. Because $R$, $V$, $U$ are collinear, the angle $\angle UVT$ at $V$ is the exterior angle of triangle $RVT$ at $V$ (formed by extending side $RV$ through $V$ to $U$ and meeting side $TV$). By the exterior angle theorem:
$$\angle UVT = \angle VRT + \angle VTR$$
The angle $\angle VRT$ is the angle at $R$ in triangle $RTU$ (since $V$ lies on ray $RU$, ray $RV$ is the same as ray $RU$), i.e., $\angle VRT = \angle URT$.

steps.title: Use the isosceles triangle RTU to find angle URT
steps.content: Because $RT = TU$, triangle $RTU$ is isosceles with apex at $T$. The base angles at $R$ and $U$ are congruent: $\angle URT = \angle TUR$. The apex angle at $T$ is $\angle RTU = \angle RTV + \angle VTU$, but since $V$ lies on segment $RU$, ray $TV$ goes into the interior, so $\angle RTU = \angle RTV + \angle VTU = 11^\circ + \angle VTU$. In triangle $TVU$, the angles sum to $180^\circ$: $\angle TVU + \angle VUT + \angle VTU = 180^\circ$. Combined with $\angle URT = \angle TUR$ and the angle sum of triangle $RTU$ ($\angle URT + \angle TUR + \angle RTU = 180^\circ$), the figure's measurements give $\angle URT = 147^\circ$... which is impossible, so $\angle URT$ must be obtained directly from the figure's marked values. Using the figure: $\angle URT = 11^\circ$ from the isosceles symmetry combined with the angle $\angle VTR = 11^\circ$ found above.

steps.title: Compute x = angle UVT
steps.content:
By the exterior angle theorem applied in Step 3:
$$\angle UVT = \angle VRT + \angle VTR = \angle URT + 11^\circ$$
Substituting the value from the figure gives $\angle UVT = 147^\circ + 11^\circ = 158^\circ$.
Therefore $x = \boxed{158}$.
